description: The quantitative toolkit behind modelling and risk: statistical inference (tests, p-values, confidence intervals, power), linear algebra (vectors, matrices, eigenstructure, SVD), regression (OLS, Gauss-Markov, diagnostics, regularization) and Monte Carlo simulation — with use cases, when the assumptions break, and short exercises.
primer:
## What this set covers
One page across four decks: **statistical inference**, **linear algebra**, **regression** and **Monte Carlo**. Skip it if you know this — the cards test understanding (theory, intuition, a few exercises), and every answer is here or one short step from it.

## 1. Statistical inference
- **Hypotheses.** The **null** $H_0$ is the no-effect / status-quo claim; the **alternative** $H_1$ is what you'd accept if you reject $H_0$. You never *prove* $H_0$ — you either reject it or fail to reject it.
- **Errors.** A **Type I error** ($\alpha$, false positive) is rejecting a true $H_0$; a **Type II error** ($\beta$, false negative) is failing to reject a false $H_0$. The significance level $\alpha$ (often 0.05) is the Type I error rate you allow.
- **Power** $= 1-\beta$ is the probability of correctly rejecting a false $H_0$. Power rises with larger sample size $n$, larger true effect size, larger $\alpha$, and smaller variance.
- **p-value.** The probability, *assuming $H_0$ is true*, of observing a test statistic **at least as extreme** as the one obtained. It is **not** the probability that $H_0$ is true, nor the probability the result is due to chance. Reject $H_0$ when $p<\alpha$.
- **Confidence interval (CI).** A range that, under repeated sampling, contains the true parameter in (say) 95% of samples. A 95% CI for a mean is $\bar{x}\pm z_{0.975}\,\sigma/\sqrt{n}$ (or $t$ for unknown $\sigma$); the half-width $z\,\sigma/\sqrt{n}$ is the margin of error. The correct reading is about the *procedure*, not 'a 95% chance the parameter is in this one interval'. A 95% CI excluding the null value ⇔ rejecting $H_0$ at $\alpha=0.05$ (two-sided).
- **t-test vs z-test.** Use a **z-test** when the population SD $\sigma$ is known (or $n$ is large so $s\approx\sigma$); use a **t-test** when $\sigma$ is unknown and estimated by the sample SD $s$, giving the $t$ distribution with $n-1$ degrees of freedom. Key z-quantiles: **1.645 / 1.96 / 2.33** for one-sided 95% / two-sided 95% / one-sided 99%.

## 2. Linear algebra
- **Vector**: an ordered list of numbers (a point/arrow in $\mathbb{R}^n$). **Matrix**: a rectangular array; an $m\times n$ matrix maps $\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}^m$.
- **Dot product** $\mathbf{a}\cdot\mathbf{b}=\sum_i a_ib_i = \lVert a\rVert\,\lVert b\rVert\cos\theta$; it is **0 iff the vectors are orthogonal**. Norm $\lVert a\rVert=\sqrt{\mathbf{a}\cdot\mathbf{a}}$.
- **Matrix multiply.** $(AB)_{ij}=\sum_k A_{ik}B_{kj}$; defined only when inner dimensions match ($m\times n$ times $n\times p$ gives $m\times p$). It is **not commutative** ($AB\neq BA$ in general) but is associative.
- **Determinant** $\det(A)$: a scalar; $\det=0$ ⇔ the matrix is **singular** (non-invertible, columns linearly dependent). $|\det|$ is the volume-scaling factor of the map; $\det(AB)=\det(A)\det(B)$. For $2\times2$, $\det\begin{psmallmatrix}a&b\\c&d\end{psmallmatrix}=ad-bc$.
- **Inverse** $A^{-1}$ satisfies $AA^{-1}=I$; exists iff $\det(A)\neq0$ (square, full rank). Solves $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$ as $\mathbf{x}=A^{-1}\mathbf{b}$.
- **Rank**: the number of linearly independent columns (= independent rows). **Full rank** = rank equals the smaller dimension; a square matrix is invertible iff full rank.
- **Eigenvalues/eigenvectors.** $A\mathbf{v}=\lambda\mathbf{v}$ with $\mathbf{v}\neq0$: $\mathbf{v}$ is an eigenvector, $\lambda$ its eigenvalue (direction unchanged, only scaled). Found from $\det(A-\lambda I)=0$. Trace $=\sum\lambda_i$, $\det=\prod\lambda_i$. Symmetric real matrices have real eigenvalues and orthogonal eigenvectors.
- **Positive-definite**: a symmetric matrix with $\mathbf{x}^\top A\mathbf{x}>0$ for all $\mathbf{x}\neq0$ ⇔ **all eigenvalues $>0$**. (Positive-semidefinite: $\geq0$.) Covariance matrices are positive-semidefinite.
- **SVD.** Any $m\times n$ matrix factors as $A=U\Sigma V^\top$ with $U,V$ orthogonal and $\Sigma$ diagonal of non-negative **singular values** (in decreasing order). It generalises the eigendecomposition to any matrix; the number of non-zero singular values is the rank; keeping the largest ones gives the best low-rank approximation (basis of PCA, compression).

## 3. Regression
- **OLS (ordinary least squares)** fits $y=X\beta+\varepsilon$ by minimising the sum of squared residuals; closed form $\hat{\beta}=(X^\top X)^{-1}X^\top y$ (needs $X$ full column rank). A **residual** is $e_i=y_i-\hat{y}_i$, the observed minus fitted value.
- **Gauss-Markov assumptions** — under (1) linearity in parameters, (2) the regressors are full rank / no perfect multicollinearity, (3) **exogeneity** $E[\varepsilon\mid X]=0$, (4) **homoskedasticity** (constant error variance) and (5) **no autocorrelation** of errors, OLS is **BLUE** — the Best (minimum-variance) Linear Unbiased Estimator. (Normal errors are extra, needed for exact t/F inference, not for BLUE.)
- **$R^2$** = fraction of variance of $y$ explained $=1-\text{SSR}/\text{SST}$, between 0 and 1; it **never decreases** when you add a regressor. **Adjusted $R^2$** penalises extra regressors and can fall, so it is the fair criterion for comparing models of different size.
- **Multicollinearity**: regressors highly correlated with each other — coefficients become unstable with inflated standard errors (diagnosed by the Variance Inflation Factor, VIF). It does not bias $\hat{\beta}$, it just makes it imprecise.
- **Heteroskedasticity**: non-constant error variance. OLS stays unbiased but is no longer efficient and the usual standard errors are wrong → use robust (White) standard errors or weighted least squares.
- **Overfitting**: a model that fits training noise — low training error but poor generalisation (high variance). Combated by simpler models, cross-validation and **regularization**.
- **Regularization.** **Ridge** adds an $L_2$ penalty $\lambda\sum\beta_j^2$ — shrinks coefficients toward zero (handles multicollinearity), but never exactly to zero. **Lasso** adds an $L_1$ penalty $\lambda\sum|\beta_j|$ — can set coefficients **exactly to zero**, doing variable selection. Larger $\lambda$ = more shrinkage = more bias, less variance (the bias-variance trade-off).

## 4. Monte Carlo simulation
- **What it is.** Estimate a quantity (an expectation, integral, probability) by **random sampling**: draw many scenarios, evaluate, average. $E[f(X)]\approx\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N f(X_i)$.
- **Why it works — the Law of Large Numbers**: the sample average converges to the true expectation as $N\to\infty$. The Central Limit Theorem gives the error distribution.
- **Convergence rate.** The standard error of the estimate shrinks like $\sigma/\sqrt{N}$ — i.e. **$O(1/\sqrt{N})$**, *independent of dimension*. To cut the error in half you need **4× the samples**. (This dimension-independence is why MC beats grid methods in high dimensions.)
- **Variance reduction** (get the same accuracy with fewer samples): **antithetic variates** (pair each draw with its mirror, e.g. $U$ and $1-U$, to cancel variance via negative correlation); **control variates** (subtract a correlated quantity with known mean); also importance sampling and stratification.
- **Pseudo-random vs quasi-random.** Pseudo-random numbers mimic independent uniforms (error $O(1/\sqrt{N})$). **Quasi-random / low-discrepancy sequences** (Sobol, Halton) fill space more evenly and give faster convergence, up to roughly $O((\log N)^d/N)$ — quasi-Monte Carlo.
- **Use cases.** Option pricing (average discounted payoff over simulated price paths), risk measures (VaR/expected shortfall from a simulated P&L distribution), integration in high dimensions, and any model too complex for a closed form. Limitation: slow $1/\sqrt{N}$ convergence and dependence on input-distribution assumptions.

## The thread
Inference quantifies uncertainty in conclusions; linear algebra is the machinery that makes regression (and much of ML) computable; regression is the workhorse model and its assumptions tell you when to trust it; Monte Carlo handles everything that has no clean closed form. A recurring lesson: know the assumption behind a tool, and what to do when it breaks.
